title: Motion Classification
subtitle: Char-wise vs line-wise vs block, inclusive vs exclusive.
summary: Every motion has two attributes: shape (char/line/block) and inclusivity (does it include the endpoint?). These attributes change how operators behave.

blocks.text: Every motion in Vim has two characteristics that determine how operators interact with it. Understanding these explains a lot of "surprising" Vim behavior.

Char-wise motion + delete = delete a range of characters. Line-wise motion + delete = delete whole lines (even partial coverage). This is why {key:d}{key:j} deletes *two whole lines* — {key:j} is line-wise.
Inclusivity: does the motion include the destination character?

blocks.title: Why It Matters
blocks.text:
{key:d}{key:f}{key:x} deletes from cursor *through* the next x. {key:d}{key:t}{key:x} deletes from cursor *up to* the next x (leaving x in place).

{key:d}{key:e} deletes through the end of the word (the last char of the word IS deleted). {key:d}{key:w} deletes through the *start* of the next word, which often leaves you with a hanging space.

**Force overrides** with {key:v}, {key:V}, {key:Ctrl-V} after the operator:

  • {key:d}{key:v}{key:j} — delete *characterwise* down (instead of the default linewise behavior of `dj`).
  • {key:d}{key:V}{key:f}{key:x} — delete *linewise* through next x.
